elements.sent-2411-001: Growing congestion in current mobile networks necessitates innovative solutions.
elements.sent-2411-002: This paper explores the potential of mmWave 5G networks in urban settings, focusing on Integrated Access and Backhaul (IAB) and the Smart Radio Environment (SRE).
elements.sent-2411-003: The mmWave traffic will be mainly made of short bursts to transfer large volumes of data and long idle periods where data are processed.
elements.sent-2411-004: This must change the way of designing mobile radio networks.
elements.sent-2411-005: To this extent, we propose network planning models leveraging the maximization of the achievable peak throughput.
elements.sent-2411-006: Results highlight the advantages of this approach during the network planning phase, providing insights into better accommodating the demands of mobile traffic without sacrificing the overall network capacity.
elements.sent-2411-007: It is projected that by 2029 \cite{ericssonReportJun2023,ericssonReportNov2023 , the overall mobile data usage will more than triple compared to 2023, and this growth will be primarily caused by 5G New Radio (NR) connections, overshadowing the utilization of previous mobile radio generations.}
elements.sent-2411-008: To address this issue, every cellular network generation has been followed by another constantly aiming at improving spectral efficiency and bandwidth of the previous wireless communication standard.
elements.sent-2411-009: In this context, allocating new spectrum portions enables the use of larger bandwidths, which straightforwardly deliver a linear increase of achievable bitrates.
elements.sent-2411-010: Following this logic, the 5th Generation (5G) standard for mobile communication of the 3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) started to consider multiple frequency ranges.
elements.sent-2411-011: Frequency Range 1 (FR1), which partially overlaps with 4G frequencies; Frequency Range 2 (FR2) or mmWaves, operating at frequencies from 24 GHz up to 70 GHz, which permits bandwidth allocations of up to 2000 MHz; Frequency Range 3 or upper mid-band, operating at frequencies from 7 to 24 GHz, which is gaining more and more momentum in the transition towards the 6th Generation (6G), but not yet included in the standard; the Terahertz Frequency Range, with frequencies above 90 GHz, which promises astonishing throughputs for the 6G, but still requires to face nontrivial technical challenges.
elements.sent-2411-012: The huge FR2 bandwidth promises a tenfold or more improvement in the bitrate.
elements.sent-2411-013: This drastic advancement paves the way for previously unattainable transmission speeds for end-users, unlocking possibilities for high-speed, low-latency applications envisioned in 5G.
elements.sent-2411-014: Simultaneously, this progress serves as a catalyst for innovation and advancement in industry and academic research environments.
elements.sent-2411-015: Consequently, mmWaves are recognized as a pivotal component in realizing the full potential of 5G and 5G-Advanced as outlined by 3GPP.
elements.sent-2411-016: The transition to mmWaves, however, comes with a set of challenges.
elements.sent-2411-017: Path loss increases with frequency, resulting in reduced network coverage.
elements.sent-2411-018: Furthermore, mmWaves interact poorly with common urban materials, experiencing remarkable penetration losses, negligible diffraction (e.g., from brick and concrete), and strong reflection (e.g., from glass and metal).
elements.sent-2411-019: As a result, FR2 communication predominantly relies on Line-of-Sight (LoS) paths.
elements.sent-2411-020: Yet, the complex urban layout can obstruct LoS between a Base Station (BS) and users, leading to the emergence of coverage blind spots.
elements.sent-2411-021: One viable approach to mitigate signal attenuation is network densification which, by deploying more BSs, aims to increase the received power per user and the probability of LoS conditions.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, trenching and cabling costs may rapidly become a big issue.
elements.sent-2411-023: To address this, 3GPP Release 16 introduced a novel approach for cellular network coverage known as Integrated Access and Backhaul (IAB)~\cite{3GPPTR38.874 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: This new paradigm employs a wireless multi-hop backhaul where the signal is relayed from BS to User Equipment (UE) via IAB nodes, preserving the need of a single cabled connections in the cell.
elements.sent-2411-025: The wireless multi-hop backhaul not only helps to mitigate path losses but also to alleviate the impact of static obstacles (like buildings) within the service area by strategically selecting paths that circumvent those obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-026: However, in urban settings, the challenges go beyond static obstacles: nomadic impediments, like vehicles and pedestrians, cause temporary obstructions on the wireless link ~\cite{8254900 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: As a candidate to provide an effective solution to the impact of nomadic obstacles, the concept of the Smart Radio Environment (SRE) has garnered substantial interest.
elements.sent-2411-028: A SRE implements the vision of a propagation environment no longer acting as an entity transmitter and receiver can only measure and adapt to, but rather a proper component of the system that can be actively tuned and utilized.
elements.sent-2411-029: The realization of an SRE implies integrating into the RAN one or many special radio devices, named Smart Radio Devices (SRD), capable of counteracting the channel impairments caused by nomadic obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-030: The two main types of SRD are: Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RISs) and Network-Controlled Repeaters (NCRs).
elements.sent-2411-031: An RIS consists in planar array of passive elements such that, by applying a proper phase shift to each element, an impinging radio wave can be reflected to the desired direction, while minimizing power dispersion and interference.
elements.sent-2411-032: RIS's appeal lies in their quasi-passive nature, functioning as passive beamformers, and thus included as a subject of study within ETSI~\cite{ETSIGRRIS001,ETSIGRRIS002,ETSIGRRIS003 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: NCRs, recently introduced in 3GPP Release 18~\cite{3GPPTR38.867 , operate, instead, in an amplify-and-forward manner.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Although entailing larger costs than an RIS, the power boost of NCR amplification offers more reliable links and faster throughputs.
elements.sent-2411-035: While numerous studies have explored the impact of these technologies at the link and system levels, only a limited number have ventured into examining their broader implications across a urban RAN deployment~\cite{Moro2021,9771934,9930587,10293769 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: In this article, we want to investigate whether the interoperation of mmWaves, IAB, RIS, and NCR can yield advantages within an urban RAN setup, and to what extent.
elements.sent-2411-037: By means of Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) network planning models we can identify and assess the optimal network layout and the best operational parameters in every potential deployment scenario.
elements.sent-2411-038: This allowed us to derive when and how this interplay can be effective, and thus provide reliable deployment guidelines to maximize the benefit/cost ratio of these devices.

elements.sent-2411-039: To better identify the effectiveness of a given network layout, we believe it is crucially important to understand how the network will be used in practice.
elements.sent-2411-040: Conventional network planning practices primarily focus on maximizing the average throughput per user~\cite{6678102 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: However, recent surveys~\cite{sandvineReport2022,sandvineReport2023 indicate a growing dominance of video traffic, estimated to account for over 80\% of global mobile traffic.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Video traffic exhibits peak, bursty characteristics~\cite{rao2011network .}
elements.sent-2411-043: At the same time, the very high throughput supported by mmWave communications allows the transfer of large volumes of bits in very short activation periods.
elements.sent-2411-044: That generates traffic patterns characterized by peak rate traffic injections followed by long idle periods to consume received data.
elements.sent-2411-045: Consequently, optimizing the achievable peak throughput per user emerges as a superior approach when designing a network capable of accommodating mmWave traffic bursts.
elements.sent-2411-046: In addition, it brings the advantage of providing a network with the intrinsic capability of showing better speed-test performance, which is a key performance indicator to assess the quality of mobile radio networks from a user perspective.
elements.sent-2411-047: This study investigates deployment guidelines for a 5G IAB-enabled mmWave RAN enriched with SRDs in an urban scenario featuring both static and nomadic obstacles, as the one in Figure~\ref{fig:Sysmod .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Results indicate that pursuing the optimization of peak throughput, as opposed to the conventional max-throughput approach, can be advantageous in better accommodating traffic bursts characterizing such networks.
elements.sent-2411-049: The remainder of this paper is structured as follows: Section~\ref{sec:related_works reviews the relevant literature related to our study, Section~\ref{sec:system_model provides an in-depth survey of the various components within the considered network scenario and their interconnections.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Section~\ref{sec:blockage_model outlines the model we propose to account for the presence of static and nomadic obstacles.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Section~\ref{sec:opt_models delves into the MILP network planning models devised for this study.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Section~\ref{sec:results discusses the results of the numerical analysis we performed.}
elements.sent-2411-053: The paper concludes with final remarks in Section~\ref{sec:conclusion .}
elements.sent-2411-054: The potential and challenges of mmWaves for mobile radio networks have been well documented in the past decade: stimulated by the availability of large bandwidths and large antenna arrays in small form-factors, ~\cite{6515173,Akdeniz2014,rappaport2014,niu2015survey have pioneered the study of mmWave propagation in indoor and urban environments and its channel modeling.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Finally, ~\cite{8700132,sakaguchi2017and discuss the integration of mmWave in the 5G NR context.}
elements.sent-2411-056: IAB in the context of mmWaves has garnered the interest of the scientific community since its inclusion in 3GPP as a study item.
elements.sent-2411-057: In~\cite{8514996,9040265 , the authors propose a full-stack ns-3 simulator of an IAB network operating at mmWaves; \cite{8882288 carry out a stochastic geometry analysis to show IAB deployments are capacity-limited by the overall traffic routed through the cabled connection.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: The authors in~\cite{9187867 investigate the performance of IAB in urban areas in terms of robustness and compare it to fiber-only and hybrid deployments, while~\cite{10.3389/frcmn.2021.647284 presents a survey on different applications of IAB with cache-enabled, optical transport, and non-terrestrial communication networks.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: In~\cite{9433519 discusses the advantages of IAB, identifying it as an easy-to-deploy solution for network densification.}
elements.sent-2411-060: RISs have recently emerged as a disruptive technology for wireless communications:~\cite{di2019smart ,~\cite{8796365 and~\cite{tsilipakos2020toward assess the revolutionary role RIS could hold in the SRE in its different modes of operation and use cases.}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Authors of~\cite{9086766 analyze the differences between reflectarrays and metasurfaces, and the challenges in their optimization and precoding, while in~\cite{9122596 attention is brought to the need for different phase-shift optimizations based on different network-level goals.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: MmWave NCRs are another recent discussion topic: in~\cite{9119122 , the authors carry out a preliminary comparison between RIS and relays, identifying the RIS size as a determinant factor to approach the throughput performance of a relay; ~\cite{10065985 underlines the importance of side control information coming from the parent gNB to allow the NCR to be a better and more efficient alternative to legacy RF repeaters.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: In the system-level analysis carried out in~\cite{da2023system , results show how the user throughput improves when NCRs are added, while intercell interference, though still present, does not cause outages;~\cite{9720231,10168854 highlights the combined use of both RIS and NCR as the best option for achieving coverage in urban scenarios.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to consider network planning in mmWave 5G FR2 settings with IAB and SRDs like RISs and NCRs.
elements.sent-2411-065: On top of that, the contributions of this article are:.
elements.sent-2411-066: A comprehensive and detailed MILP network planning optimization model, taking into account device installation, traffic routing, IAB multi-hop radio relaying, SRDs and capacity impairments due to obstacles;.
elements.sent-2411-067: Different objective functions tailored to compare the standard, baseline approach of max-throughput maximization to the proposed peak-throughput optimization;.
elements.sent-2411-068: A probabilistic blockage model integrating both self-blockage and nomadic obstacles in the cell to synthesize the probability of being in a specific state of blockage;.
elements.sent-2411-069: A 3D static blockage scenario derived from real data of the building distribution of the city of Milan, Italy.
elements.sent-2411-070: We are considering a 5G NR IAB network operating at mmWave frequencies, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:Sysmod , that includes: \begin{enumerate* [label={(\roman*) ] \item User Equipments (UE), i.e., hand-held devices such as smartphones or tablets, \item a unique IAB Donor, operating as a complete Base Station (BS) and the sole node in the RAN wired to the core network, \item IAB Nodes, which are simplified BS units capable of providing UEs with access and wirelessly relaying data to/from other IAB Nodes.}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: We assume a tree-like IAB backhaul topology, as recommended in 3GPP specifications~\cite{3GPPTR38.874 to facilitate multi-hop end-to-end connections between the IAB Donor and UEs.}
elements.sent-2411-072: An asymmetric Time-Division Duplexing (TDD) DL-UL configuration 4:1 is employed, which is common to every 5G NR deployment.
elements.sent-2411-073: This configuration allows a maximum allocation of 80\% of frame resources for downlink data flows (from the network to the UEs), and up to 20\% for the uplink ones (from the UEs to the network).
elements.sent-2411-074: Narrow beamforming is available across all links to enhance signal propagation in the mmWave range.
elements.sent-2411-075: This, combined with the half-duplex transmissions at each device and the consistent application of Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) , ensures that mutual interference is generally kept to a negligible level.
elements.sent-2411-076: Consequently, the assumption is made that interference between different links is null, as in other works in the literature~\cite{devoti2020,7342886 .}
elements.sent-2411-077: The incorporation of SRDs in an IAB Network is primarily driven by their ability to influence electromagnetic propagation to enhance channel conditions in the presence of obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-078: Operating as passive beamformers, RISs can effectively redirect incident radio signals toward specific directions.
elements.sent-2411-079: In contrast, NCRs are not passive; they possess the capability to amplify the received signal before retransmitting it to the intended receiver.
elements.sent-2411-080: However, at the system level, they play a role similar to RIS, albeit with a different implementation.
elements.sent-2411-081: Structurally, RISs consist of planar surfaces of many small radiating elements.
elements.sent-2411-082: The manipulation of phase shifts at each element enables the constructive alignment of incoming waves toward the intended direction and, at the same time, the capability to mitigate interference in other directions.
elements.sent-2411-083: Importantly, this process involves no power transmission; rather, it leverages the reflection of the received power.
elements.sent-2411-084: This is where the core aspect of passive beamformer lies.
elements.sent-2411-085: On the contrary, NCR functions as an active amplify-and-forward relay, employing two back-to-back tunable phased-array panels capable of full-duplex operation\footnote{Full-duplex must be intended for the entire device as a whole: one panel can receive while the other transmits.}
elements.sent-2411-086: The same panel cannot simultaneously transmit and receive.. .
elements.sent-2411-087: NCRs enhance the radio propagation environment by facilitating high-gain Non-Line-of-Sight (NLoS) paths between UEs and IAB Nodes.
elements.sent-2411-088: Given their amplification capability, NCRs generally offer higher bit rates compared to RISs.
elements.sent-2411-089: Similar to RISs, NCRs can be deployed to establish alternative paths for UEs, involving reflection and amplification.
elements.sent-2411-090: Despite some differences, similar operational constraints related to their hardware limitations apply to both devices.
elements.sent-2411-091: RISs are defined by a specific field of view (FoV) within which both incoming and reflected radio wave directions must be confined.
elements.sent-2411-092: In the case of NCRs, when considering a single data flow direction (either downlink or uplink), one panel is consistently active as a transmitter, while the other concurrently operates as a receiver.
elements.sent-2411-093: Therefore, one panel (BS panel) is always perfectly oriented toward the parent BS, while the other one (UE panel) can serve UEs located in its FoV, similarly to an RIS.
elements.sent-2411-094: Full-duplex NCR transmissions are the results of recent technological advancements based on sophisticated interference cancellation and isolation techniques between the two panels.
elements.sent-2411-095: This introduces some limitations regarding the reciprocal orientation of the two NCR panels.
elements.sent-2411-096: UEs can be served through either a direct link from an IAB Node (or the IAB Donor) or a Smart Radio Connection (SRC): a triplet involving a UE, an IAB Node, and an SRD (either an RIS or an NCR).
elements.sent-2411-097: IAB Nodes are expected to dynamically control and modify SRC configurations~\cite{9720231 , activating the reflection through the SRD when obstacles obstruct the direct link.}
elements.sent-2411-098: This improves the network's resilience to blockages caused by obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-099: The activation is based on SNR: when the selected link exhibits a lower SNR than its alternative, the serving BS dynamically switches its beam to the alternative link: this policy is named max-selection.
elements.sent-2411-100: Note that, as in any radio planning approach, we do not delve into the details of link recovery operations.
elements.sent-2411-101: We assume, as discussed in the literature, that this mechanism is in place and effective\footnote{Note that efficient losses of this mechanism can be modeled by equivalent capacity losses in the access links.. .}
elements.sent-2411-102: The mmWave channel model and path loss model, as presented in~\cite{10168854 , incorporates the effects of RIS and NCR, along with involved IAB Donor, IAB Nodes, and UEs.}

elements.sent-2411-104: Links connecting IAB Nodes (or IAB Donor) to other IAB nodes or SRDs, given the height of their sites, are only affected by static obstacles (e.g., buildings) and not by nomadic and random ground-level obstacles, like vehicles and pedestrians.
elements.sent-2411-105: Consequently, when LoS is available between those two types of devices, the associated bitrate is considered stable.
elements.sent-2411-106: Vice versa, links terminated in UEs, both direct (from an IAB Node) and reflected/relayed (from an SRD), are affected by static and nomadic obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-107: A detailed blockage model of these links, together with its impact on the average link bitrate, is provided in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-108: A precise representation of the obstacles and their impact on link blockage becomes essential for planning and optimizing the deployment of mmWave RANs in order to ensure that the outputs of the analysis are consistent with real-life scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-109: To model the occurrence of link blockage, it is important to distinguish between three categories of blockages: \begin{enumerate* [label={(\roman*) ] \item blockages due to static obstacles, \item blockages due to nomadic obstacles, and \item self-blockage, \end{enumerate* which we detail in the next paragraphs.}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: Static obstacles are typically represented by buildings or building structures.
elements.sent-2411-111: Their characterization is deterministic and relies on the geometry of the scenario under consideration, i.e., the positions of buildings and, on a smaller scale, street furniture; this information can be extracted from digital maps and pre-processed to identify areas where links are obstructed or unobstructed.
elements.sent-2411-112: An example of such a process is shown in Figure~\ref{fig:building_processing : an infinite blockage loss is considered whenever a link intersects a building's polyhedron.}
elements.sent-2411-113: This choice is justified by the high penetration loss of mmWave radio signals, exacerbated by the thickness of a building and the additional obstacles inside its volume.
elements.sent-2411-114: As a result, not only the identification of static blockage is deterministic, but also the link availability: all links crossing a static obstacle are considered unreliable and can be pruned in pre-processing, substantially reducing the solution space of the network planner.

elements.sent-2411-115: Blockages due to nomadic obstacles are caused by moving vehicles or pedestrians and are modeled using stochastic geometry methods~\cite{6290250,10168854 .}
elements.sent-2411-116: When a nomadic obstacle blocks a link's LoS, the blockage loss can be computed with a modified version of the 3GPP blockage loss model~\cite{GRANW2018study .}
elements.sent-2411-117: The blockage attenuation introduced by human blockage can reach as high as 20 dB if the mmWave link is subject to multiple human obstructions.
elements.sent-2411-118: However, the received signal quality may still be reliable enough to meet service requirements.
elements.sent-2411-119: For this reason, even if the signal is attenuated, the radio link can still be available for service and must be considered as part of a viable solution.
elements.sent-2411-120: We rely on an improved version of the Blockage Model B in 3GPP TR 38.901 document~\cite{GRANW2018study , which accurately characterizes the blockage loss affecting a direct communication link by means of geometric considerations.}
elements.sent-2411-121: Specifically, each of the K potential obstacles, or blockers, obstructing the direct link between transmitter (Tx) and receiver (Rx) is modeled as a rectangular screen with height $h_k$ and width $w_k$.
elements.sent-2411-122: The blockage attenuation is computed according to a knife-edge diffraction model applied to the rectangle.
elements.sent-2411-123: Differently from 3GPP version, though, which only considers a two-dimensional geometry, the improved version considers a three-dimensional representation, which is more accurate when the blocker’s length cannot be ignored, f.i., in case of vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-124: This improved obstacle model is then implemented within a Monte-Carlo simulator in which random obstacles are dropped in a certain circle around the Tx: the centers of the blockers are distributed according to a homogeneous Poisson point process (PPP) on the 2D circle with radius $R$ in the $x-y$ plane with density $\lambda_B$, which denotes the mean number of blockers per square meter.
elements.sent-2411-125: The orientation of the blockers $\varrho_B$ is uniformly distributed at random, i.e., $\varrho_B \sim \mathcal{U [0, 2\pi)$.}
elements.sent-2411-126: The blockage probability given the Tx-Rx distance $R$ is given by the number of blockage events over the total number of events considered.
elements.sent-2411-127: The blockage probability as a function of the distance $R$ turns out to follow an empirical exponential law, and thus, an efficient and reasonable way to compute the blockage probability for any distance is to fit the exponential curve and interpolate it for the desired distance.
elements.sent-2411-128: The blockage probability due to nomadic obstacles, for specific obstacle size and density, can be written as: \begin{equation}P_B(R)=1 - e^{(-\beta R)} \label{eq:fitted_exp}\end{equation} where $\beta$ is a positive real number.
elements.sent-2411-129: This behavior also complies with work in~\cite{6290250,6840343 .}
elements.sent-2411-130: The blockage loss CDF can be very accurately fitted by a Gaussian mixture model of two Gaussian distributions, where the reciprocal weight depends on the height difference between blocker and BS.
elements.sent-2411-131: Given its extreme proximity to the UE, the user body must be treated as a special obstacle that needs a dedicated approach to be correctly modeled.
elements.sent-2411-132: More precisely, the primary sources of blockage are the user's hand holding the device (e.g., a smartphone) and the user's body obstructing the LoS link to the serving BS~\cite{raghavan2019statistical .}

elements.sent-2411-141: In a conventional single link access scenario, where the UE receives service through a direct link to the BS, the probability of this link lying within the SBR (denoted as event A) is given by: \begin{equation}P(A) = P_{p}\left(\frac{x_{sb}^p}{2 \pi}\right) + P_{l}\left(\frac{x_{sb}^l}{2 \pi}\right) \label{eq:direct_sbr}\end{equation} Here, $P_p$ represents the probability of the user holding the UE in portrait mode, and $P_l = 1 - P_p$ is the probability of the user holding the UE in landscape mode.
elements.sent-2411-142: In a scenario where an SRD is present, a UE can be served by either the direct link or the link passing through the SRD, we refer to it as indirect link.
elements.sent-2411-143: In this case, the probability the indirect link lies within the SBR, given the direct link is in SBR as well, (Event B) depends on the angular distance between direct and indirect link .
elements.sent-2411-144: The probability of the event B is: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} P(B) =~ P_{p}\left(\max\left(\frac{x_{sb}^p - \Delta\phi}{x_{sb}^p},0\right)\right)+ ~P_{l}\left(\max\left(\frac{x_{sb}^l - \Delta\phi}{x_{sb}^l},0\right)\right) \label{eq:refl_sbr} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\Delta\phi \in [0,\pi]$ represents the minimum angular distance between the direct and indirect link.
elements.sent-2411-145: When $\Delta\phi=0$, indicating co-located links, the probability of the indirect link being blocked is $1$.
elements.sent-2411-146: As the angular spread $\Delta\phi$ increases, the indirect link leaves the SBR, and thus, the probability approaches zero.

elements.subsection-2411-004: Blockage probability due to non-static obstacles
elements.sent-2411-148: Leveraging the blockage probabilities outlined in Section~\ref{sub:nomadic and Section~\ref{sub:self , we formulate a comprehensive blockage probability model encompassing both nomadic blockage and self-blockage.}}
elements.sent-2411-149: Indeed, differently from static obstacles, nomadic obstacles and self-blockage require a probabilistic analysis of the blockage conditions.
elements.sent-2411-150: In addition, the simultaneous occurrence of both blockage types has a stronger impact than the case a single blockage arises, as the attenuation of the two obstacles is additive.

elements.sent-2411-161: We believe that a thorough investigation of the optimal network planning solutions in several different scenarios can provide a deep understanding of the phenomena impacting on optimal choices, which enables the design of effective network deployment guidelines.
elements.sent-2411-162: For this reason, accurately modeling the behavior and the limitations of all network players is pivotal to the entire study.
elements.sent-2411-163: In this work we deal with two distinct MILP models that rely on the same network setup, but focus on different planning objectives: one is the Mean-Throughput Formulation (MTF), a baseline formulation where the objective function is the maximization of the overall mean user throughput, the other is the Peak-Throughput Formulation (PTF), an extension of the baseline approach where the maximization addresses the peak user throughput.
elements.sent-2411-164: Both formulations share a common notation, defined as follows.
elements.sent-2411-165: We define a set $\C$ of Candidate Sites (CS), where a network device (the IAB Donor, an IAB Node, an RIS, or an NCR) can be deployed to cover a planned mmWave network in an urban area.
elements.sent-2411-166: Within $\C$, two CSs, $\hat{c}$ and $\Tilde{c}$, are designated for the installation of two special devices: $\hat{c}$ is reserved for the IAB Donor, while $\Tilde{c}$ serves as a CS for a "fake" SRD, representing a null SRD.
elements.sent-2411-167: This approach lets the solver to determine whether to assign to each UE a real SRC (a BS, a UE, and an SRD) or a "fake" SRC (a BS, a UE, and the "fake" SRD), thus the availability of the sole direct link.

elements.sent-2411-239: In this formulation, we want to model the bursty nature of the mmWave traffic.
elements.sent-2411-240: Given an achievable peak rate much higher than mean rate, mmWave data transfers will be very short, working at Gbps.
elements.sent-2411-241: In this context, the probability that two short bursts occur at the same time can be reasonably considered negligible, therefore the peak throughput of each burst can be assumed to individually occupy the entire set of resources left unused by the mean-throughput traffic, in practice, as it was isolated from other bursts.
elements.sent-2411-242: The Peak-Throughput Formulation (PTF) extends the Mean-Throughput Formulation (MTF) by introducing additional variables and constraints.
elements.sent-2411-243: Indeed, the allocation of peak-throughput traffic requires a modeling approach different from the one of the mean-throughput traffic in MTF, where every UE shares all available resources.
elements.sent-2411-244: Additional flow variables, $f_{t,c,d ^{\text{X,UL }$ and $f_{t,c,d ^{\text{X,DL }$, are introduced to capture the peak-throughput traffic in the wireless backhaul, while $g_{t,c,r,s ^\text{X,DL}$ and $g_{t,c,r,s ^\text{X,UL}$ represent peak-throughput access traffic .}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-246: The rationale behind PTF is to optimize the mean-throughput traffic flow distribution to increase the capacity of the bottlenecks that will be saturated as soon as peak-rate bursts start.
elements.sent-2411-247: The objective function and constraints characterizing PTF are: { { \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\max \sum_{\substack{t \in \T, c,r \in \C,s \in \SD}}\frac{g_{\substack{t,c, r,s}}^{\text{X,DL}}}{D^{\text{DL}}} + \frac{g_{\substack{t,c, r,s}}^{\text{X,UL}}}{D^{\text{UL}}},\label{milp:of_ptf}\end{equation} { Objective function: The objective function for the PTF, as expressed in (\ref{milp:of_ptf ), aims to maximize the sum of users' peak throughputs considering both uplink and downlink.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-249: The larger complexity introduced by the PTF required an MILP model that proved challenging to solve in a time window of 60 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-250: Consequently, in order to tackle larger instances and analyze many instances for each parameter setting, we have implemented a heuristic approach to speed up the solution process while maintaining high-quality solutions.
elements.sent-2411-251: In this context, two changes have been applied to the MILP solution process.
elements.sent-2411-252: Firstly, we have applied a technique, which we introduced in ~\cite{10293769 , that consists in reducing the parameter $M$, the capacity of the best backhaul link of the IAB Donor.}
elements.sent-2411-253: We found out that adjusting it to a fraction of its actual value, thus creating a bottleneck effect, allows to decrease the solution time down to few seconds with an optimality gap smaller than 5\%.
elements.sent-2411-254: Secondly, we have increased the optimality gap between the best feasible solution and the continuous relaxation upper bound from $5\%$ (used in MTF) to $40\%$.
elements.sent-2411-255: This adjustment is motivated by the behavior of the solution exploration process, which identifies feasible solutions in the first seconds and, most of the times, spends the remaining time in improving the continuous relaxation, without remarkably increasing the quality of found solutions.
elements.sent-2411-256: While this adjustment theoretically provides a lower bound of the exact optimal solution, the results presented in Section~\ref{sec:results indicate a large PTF performance improvement compared to MTF.}
elements.sent-2411-257: In this section, we analyze the optimal solutions provided by the MILP solver for the two formulations presented in Section~\ref{sec:opt_models .}
elements.sent-2411-258: We aim at comparing the outcomes of the two optimization approaches in order to understand the key features of resulting topologies and SRD deployment strategies.
elements.sent-2411-259: The set of 100 considered instances are based on hexagonal cell areas with a radius of $150$ meters, located a random points in a map (including streets and 3D building polyhedra) of the Milan urban area~\cite{MilanoGeoportale .}
elements.sent-2411-260: Instances have been selected in a heterogeneous manner, considering the diversity of building clutter, to ensure a representation of both open spaces (such as squares and parks) and urban canyons (like densely populated residential areas).
elements.sent-2411-261: Indeed, this approach allows to evaluate the impact of a realistic building distribution characterizing a typical European metropolitan city.
elements.sent-2411-262: Each cell has an IAB Donor located at the leftmost vertex of the hexagonal area, while set cardinalities $|\C|=25$, $|\T|=15$ and $|\SD|=2$ represent CSs, TPs and types of SRDs, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-263: The coordinates of the elements of these sets are randomly selected in the cell area, avoiding the space inside buildings.

elements.sent-2411-286: This campaign aims to assess the impact of the deployment budget on the optimal network design and its performance.
elements.sent-2411-287: In order to carry out the comparison, we set a minimum guaranteed user demand to $120$ Mb/s in downlink and $30$ Mb/s in uplink.
elements.sent-2411-288: Throughout the figures of this campaign, a black vertical line at budget value equal to $10$ highlights the data intersection with the next campaign in Sec.~\ref{sub:demand .}
elements.sent-2411-289: A first experimental outcome is that no solution can be found when the available budget is less than $1$ unit: the IAB Donor without the support of at least one IAB Node is not enough to guarantee the minimum throughput requirements.

elements.sent-2411-293: RISs and NCRs, instead, experience limited usage.
elements.sent-2411-294: There are few instances with deployed RISs, and an NCR is installed approximately every other instance.
elements.sent-2411-295: The reason behind this behavior is related to the throughput maximization objective, which implies a maximization of the (mean or peak) access capacity provided to each UE.

elements.sent-2411-301: In each network, we measure both the mean and the peak user throughput.
elements.sent-2411-302: Given that each formulation optimizes one of two metrics, it is important to appropriately handle the other metric to ensure a valid comparison of the results obtained from the two formulations.
elements.sent-2411-303: Specifically, for MTF, which optimizes the average throughput, the peak per-user throughput is determined by identifying the spare capacity on the bottleneck link for each specific user.
elements.sent-2411-304: For PTF, which optimizes the peak throughput, the mean throughput is computed by running an additional optimization using the MTF model, where only optimal routing and resource allocation are optimized within the network topology resulting from PTF.
elements.sent-2411-305: This approach results in instances evaluated not only in terms of the maximization objective of their own formulation but also according to the objective of the other formulation.
elements.sent-2411-306: A notable observation is that, despite PTF not specifically optimizing the mean throughput, it exhibits results remarkably similar to MTF ($200$ Mb/s in downlink and $50$ Mb/s in uplink).
elements.sent-2411-307: Nevertheless, PTF significantly outperforms MTF in terms of achievable peak throughput, with an improvement of up to $300$ Mb/s in downlink and $90$ Mb/s in uplink.
elements.sent-2411-308: Finally, all throughput curves show a saturation plateau after a specific budget level.
elements.sent-2411-309: This is caused by the the presence of a unique connection to the wired network, e.g., the IAB Donor, which is source and destination of all traffic flows and inherently presents a limited capacity of its wireless connections.
elements.sent-2411-310: When this limit is reached, deploying additional devices does not bring significant user throughput improvements.
elements.figure-2411-013: [H] \subfloat[Number of hops from IAB Donor to UE]{\includegraphics[width=0.45\columnwidth]{contents/figures/new_pictures/multihop_budget.pdf} \label{fig:mh_b}} \hfil \subfloat[Donor's node degree]{\includegraphics[width=0.45\columnwidth]{contents/figures/new_pictures/donor_degree_budget.pdf} \label{fig:dg_b}} \caption{Variation of some topological properties over budget increase.} \label{fig:topo_b}
elements.sent-2411-311: The two formulations aim at the user throughput maximization from two different perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-312: The MTF's goal is to increase the average throughput of the entire set of concurrent user flows under a minimum guaranteed per-user throughput.
elements.sent-2411-313: PTF, instead, designs a layout that provides the same minimum guaranteed throughput to the concurrent user flows, but leaves a large spare capacity in each link so that individual traffic bursts can be accommodated at the highest rate possible.
elements.sent-2411-314: These two objectives are pursued at equal deployment budget.
elements.sent-2411-315: The two perspectives result into different topological choices, which emerge from Figure~\ref{fig:mh_b , indicating the number of hops from the IAB Donor to each UE in downlink and uplink, and from Figure~\ref{fig:dg_b , showing the IAB Donor's node degree, i.e., the number of links incident to the IAB Donor.}}
elements.sent-2411-316: With a small budget, the IAB backhaul is very limited and the traffic is mainly collected by direct connections to the IAB Donor.
elements.sent-2411-317: This leads to few backhaul hops and a high IAB Donor's degree.
elements.sent-2411-318: However, as the budget increases, the access burden shifts towards IAB Nodes.
elements.sent-2411-319: This means larger backhaul networks and few connections departing from the IAB Donor towards IAB Nodes, which, in turn, connect to UEs.
elements.sent-2411-320: The difference between MTF and PTF network layouts lies in the offset between these curves.
elements.sent-2411-321: MTF tends to form deeper wireless backhaul networks than PTF, characterized by a larger number of hops from the IAB Donor to each UEs.
elements.sent-2411-322: At the same time, the IAB Donor tends to have fewer incident links in MTF than in PTF.
elements.sent-2411-323: These metrics reveal how a peak-throughput optimization requires star-like topologies to reduce potential bottleneck points.
elements.sent-2411-324: In contrast, mean-throughput can be maximized with a properly-designed deeper backhaul, with less bottleneck concern.
elements.sent-2411-325: Figure~\ref{fig:bn_b helps to shed some light on bottleneck causes.}
elements.sent-2411-326: The figure indicates which device or link acts as a bottleneck for each user flow.
elements.sent-2411-327: At small budgets, the topology mainly consists of a single IAB Donor and few IAB nodes, therefore most UEs see a flow cap imposed by the saturation of IAB Donor's and IAB Nodes' access resources.
elements.sent-2411-328: When the budget increases more IAB nodes are installed, therefore the bottleneck distribution changes: only IAB Nodes' access links become the predominant source of bottlenecks.
elements.sent-2411-329: Finally, from mid to high budgets, notice how the PTF formulation can design a backhaul that leads to the saturation of IAB Donor's backhaul links.

elements.sent-2411-354: This study addresses the growing congestion in current mobile radio networks by investigating the potential of mmWave 5G networks in urban settings.
elements.sent-2411-355: It considers the way mmWave will change the interactions between users and networks, which, thanks to multi-Gbps wireless links, will be characterized by peak throughputs much larger than the average required per-user throughput.
elements.sent-2411-356: Therefore, the traffic will be mainly made of short bursts to transfer large volume of data and long idle periods to process them, as the dominance of video traffic in current mobile networks already shows.
elements.sent-2411-357: The proposed MILP network planning models optimize operational parameters, emphasizing the maximization of achievable peak throughput and including the most recent mmWave paradigms of Integrated Access and Backhauling (IAB) and Smart Radio Environment (SRE).
elements.sent-2411-358: Results highlight the advantages of focusing on peak throughput during the network planing phase, providing insights into better accommodating the demands of mobile traffic without sacrificing the overall network capacity.
elements.sent-2411-359: This research contributes novel perspectives for the strategic deployment of mmWave 5G networks in urban scenarios, offering a basis for ongoing enhancements and refinements in mobile communication networks.

elements.sent-2411-369: He works on networking topics, his main research activities include radio resource management and optimization in wireless networks, programmable networks, and smart radio environments.
